text.title: Supply Chain Risk — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for the 7-signal library-risk checklist and the report it produces.

rule.statement: Every report MUST score 7 signals: (1) LICENSE, (2) MAINTAINER-COUNT, (3) LAST-RELEASE-AGE, (4) CVE-HISTORY-12MO, (5) STARS-TO-DOWNLOAD-RATIO, (6) TRANSITIVE-DEPTH, (7) VENDOR-LOCK-IN. Skipping a signal forces `score: NULL` for that axis; aggregate score cannot be computed and the report is marked `incomplete`.

rationale.source: Sonatype State of the Software Supply Chain 2023 / OpenSSF Scorecard

rule.rationale: Single-signal evaluation misses every major supply-chain incident of 2018-2024. The 7 signals cover the failure surface; aggregation is robust to one false reading on any single axis.

rule.statement: Data for CVE-HISTORY, LAST-RELEASE, and MAINTAINER-COUNT must be retrieved within 24h of report emission. Cached data > 24h triggers `data_freshness: stale` and downgrades confidence.

rationale.source: OSV.dev publishes new advisories daily; xz-utils CVE-2024-3094 (March 2024) was published < 48h after discovery

rule.rationale: Stale CVE data is the single most common cause of false-clean evaluations. 24h is the maximum tolerable staleness for security-relevant data.

rule.statement: LICENSE signal scores 0 (allow) for any SPDX ID in operator's allowlist, 2 (warn) for GPL-family or non-listed permissive, 3 (block) for unrecognised / proprietary / SSPL / BUSL. Custom licenses ALWAYS score 3 — even if they look permissive.

rationale.source: SPDX License List + FSF / OSI compliance practice

rule.rationale: Custom licenses cost more in legal review than they save in functionality. SSPL/BUSL specifically block commercial SaaS use; using them in a SaaS product is a launch-blocker.

rule.statement: If direct dependency adds > 50 transitive packages (npm) or > 25 (PyPI), TRANSITIVE-DEPTH scores ≥ 2. Each transitive multiplies CVE exposure.

rationale.source: leftpad incident (2016) + dependency-graph studies (Decan et al., 2019)

rule.rationale: A single direct dep dragging in 200 transitives is a structural risk; the operator cannot reasonably audit all of them. Penalising depth pushes evaluation toward lighter alternatives.

rule.statement: STARS-TO-DOWNLOAD-RATIO scores ≥ 2 (warn) when GitHub stars > 10× monthly registry downloads. High stars + low downloads = curiosity, not adoption — the maintainer support model is weak.

rationale.source: npmjs/registry-data + GitHub-stars cohort analysis (tj-actions/changed-files (2025) incident, event-stream (2018))

rule.rationale: "Popular on GitHub" and "used in production" are different signals. Tj-actions and event-stream had high stars but low real-adoption review, enabling compromise.

example.title: Signal score anchors (0-3 each)
example.code:
LICENSE : 0 MIT/Apache/BSD | 2 GPL | 3 SSPL/proprietary MAINTAINER-COUNT : 0 ≥5 | 1 3-4 | 2 2 | 3 1 maintainer LAST-RELEASE-AGE : 0 ≤90d | 1 ≤180d | 2 ≤365d | 3 >365d CVE-HISTORY-12MO : 0 none | 1 1 low | 2 1 high | 3 ≥2 high STARS-TO-DOWNLOAD-RATIO : 0 <1× | 1 1-10× | 2 10-50× | 3 >50× TRANSITIVE-DEPTH : 0 <10 | 1 10-25 | 2 25-50 | 3 >50 VENDOR-LOCK-IN : 0 standard API | 1 ecosystem-coupled | 2 proprietary protocol | 3 hosted-only SDK
Aggregate = sum (range 0-21) ≤ 6 → GREEN (adopt) 7-12 → YELLOW (adopt with pin + review) ≥ 13 → RED (decline OR fork-pin)
example.note: Threshold tuning is operator-specific; defaults match OpenSSF Scorecard low/critical bands.

example.title: Stars-only evaluation (r1+r5 violations)
example.code:
"Library has 18k stars, must be good." → adopt.
The xz-utils story: high trust signal from years of stars, single maintainer for 24 months, malicious patch landed via social engineering on the under-resourced solo. The 7-signal table would have flagged: MAINTAINER-COUNT=3, CVE-HISTORY=3, LAST-RELEASE=2.
example.note: Illustrates r1 + r5. Stars are evidence of attention, not safety.
